teamObjective.explanation: At the opening lead I hold four sequential spades with no trump. The best team use of this hand is to expend the weakest card first, keeping the 6 (and 4/5) available for later tricks or to regain the lead.

partnerBelief.summary: My partner holds three unseen cards and has made no public plays, so I cannot infer a specific suit or strength signal. Probabilistically they may be able to cover a low single, but that remains uncertain.

opponentBelief.summary: Each opponent holds three unseen cards. With the deck structure, it is plausible at least one opponent can beat a single 3 with a higher card or trump, but no specific hidden card can be treated as a fact.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 is the lowest-cost probe. It keeps the 6 as my strongest non-trump card for a later trick and lets my partner potentially win the trick without me committing a more valuable card.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 6 first would expose my best non-trump card now. If an opponent beats it with a trump or higher rank, I am left with only 3-4-5 and no clear way to regain control.
The 5 is still an intermediate card with no special trick-taking power. Playing it early wastes a resource without giving any more useful information than the 3 would provide.
Although the 4 is only slightly higher, the same logic applies: any card I lead now is likely beatable, so I should lead the absolute minimum and preserve all higher cards.

risks: An opponent may beat the 3 with a higher card or trump and take the lead.
My partner may be unable to cover the 3, leaving our team without initiative.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By retaining the 6 and the intermediate 4/5, I keep a realistic path to win a future trick or block an opponent. A low opening lead minimizes immediate resource loss.

notes: Public history contains only the new_game event, so all partner and opponent inferences are probabilistic and grounded solely in hand counts and known deck structure.
